topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit Elias like a physical blow. He stood at the edge of the clearing, his hands clenched so tightly his knuckles were white. Where the towering oak, his childhood monument, had stood, there was only a raw, jagged scar in the undergrowth. A hollowed-out stump remained, stark against the muted autumn colors. He sank onto the moss, the cold seeping instantly through his thin jacket. He stared at the missing trunk, a profound emptiness echoing the hollowness in his chest. "Gone," he whispered, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet woods. He didn't move to touch the rough wood; instead, he just let his gaze drift over the empty space, a heavy, leaden feeling settling deep in his stomach.

[story 2]

Sarah traced the outline of the bare ground with the toe of her worn boot. The silence here was oppressive, a thick blanket muffling any sound except the frantic drumming of her own pulse. She remembered the meticulous care she’d taken planting that sapling decades ago, the hopeful watering, the whispered promises to the young tree. Now, only stumps remained, evidence of a brutal, careless clearing. A shudder ran through her, a deep, involuntary tremor that started in her core and radiated outward. She closed her eyes, pressing the heels of her hands against her temples, trying to physically hold the swirling uncertainty back. "It just... vanished," she murmured, her voice barely audible, a dry rasp catching in her throat.

[story 3]

Mark walked the perimeter slowly, his steps heavy and dragging through the fallen leaves. Each step seemed to pull him further into the gloom of the woods, a weight pressing down on his shoulders that made breathing shallow. When he finally reached the spot, the sight of the severed base made him stop dead. It wasn't just a tree; it was a tangible piece of his past, violently erased. He let out a long, slow breath, the air tasting metallic and stale. His shoulders slumped forward, a visible surrender to the sheer scale of the loss. He didn't cry; he just stood there, utterly still, the world narrowing down to the stark reality of what was no longer there.
